Layout Principles:
* Put reading-first content in the left/main column: introductions, descriptions, markdown, homework content, and comments.
* Put structured facts in the right/side column: identifiers, metadata, dates, counts, actions, mini calendars, and quick links.
* On mobile, stack in that order.
* Reuse existing grids, cards, borders, muted text, tabs, and buttons.
* Popups with discussion use details on the left and discussion on the right on desktop; stack on mobile.
List Table:
* Used for discovery lists such as courses, sections, and teachers.
* Primary names follow the active locale; an available alternate localized name is secondary text.
* Structured fields support comparison.
* Rows link to detail pages.
* Across viewport widths, one prominent search remains directly available, advanced filters open in an accessible sheet, and active filters remain removable above results.
* Compact layouts use cards that avoid page-level horizontal scrolling; wide desktop layouts keep every comparison-table column inside the results container without horizontal scrolling.
* Pagination uses URL-driven Previous, page, and Next links while preserving filters.
Data Table Cells:
* Desktop data-table rows keep a consistent compact height; optional secondary lines reserve space when absent.
* Overflowing text is truncated, with its full value available from a tooltip on pointer hover and keyboard focus.
* Course and section codes use plain monospace text, never badge styling.
* Header labels and cell content share the same column edge; textual and categorical values align left, while numeric measures and counts align right.
* Blank placeholders are hidden from assistive technology and do not replace meaningful labels such as unknown or TBD.
* Numeric, status, control, and action cells do not receive truncation tooltips when their content fits.
Detail Hero:
* Used at the top of course, section, and teacher detail pages.
* Contains breadcrumb, h1, and an optional subtitle.
* h1 uses the primary display name of the current object, not an internal ID.
* At mobile widths, stable public identifiers and concise metadata precede a responsive title so long localized names do not crowd out context or actions.
Model Property Priority: Properties are ranked as primary, secondary, or tertiary so web, REST, and MCP surfaces present task-relevant information consistently.
Model Property Priority - Primary: Primary properties identify the object or answer the immediate task: display title/name, distinguishing code, due/departure/start time, status, and primary action state. Put them in h1, card titles, the first list column, or the first line of compact assistant output.
Model Property Priority - Secondary: Secondary properties support disambiguation and comparison: localized secondary name, semester, campus/location, teacher/department, counts, and short metadata. Use muted text, badges, side columns, or compact nested summaries.
Model Property Priority - Tertiary: Tertiary properties support audit or exact reconstruction: internal IDs, raw timestamps, platform metadata, long nested relations, and repeated parent objects. Hide or de-emphasize them in ordinary UI; MCP default output may omit, redact, or compact them.
Model Property Priority - Surfaces: REST may expose fuller records for application clients. MCP default output preserves primary and important secondary fields, simplifies repeated low-priority fields, and reserves exact raw payloads for explicit full mode where supported; the deprecated summary input is an alias for default.
Basic Info Card:
* Used in detail page sidebars or collapsible panels.
* Displays structured fields; does not carry comment or homework interactions.
Context Tabs:
* Used for multi-context switching within detail pages.
* Common combinations: sections — homework, calendar, comments; courses — sections, comments; teachers — sections, comments.
* Context navigation is a horizontal, touch-scrollable row with an overflow affordance on mobile and a vertical sidebar on larger screens; the active destination remains programmatically identified and scrolls into view.
Calendar Export Dialog:
* Used to display single-object iCal and personal subscription iCal.
* Presented as a copyable link; users are not required to manually construct the URL.
Shell Layout:
* Desktop sidebar and main content scroll independently.
* Signed-in desktop navigation keeps task links in Workspace; Explore and Admin tools are progressive groups.
* A destination row is either a link or a disclosure, never both.
* Signed-in mobile navigation fixes Today, Calendar, Tasks, and Explore above content and reserves its height and safe area.
* Language and theme stay in the topbar; signed-in profile actions stay in the sidebar footer.
* Anonymous topbars retain Sign in.
* Mobile topbar/navigation controls are at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels.
* Selected system/light/dark themes apply before first paint; system follows live OS changes with a no-JavaScript fallback.
Workspace Footer Policy:
* Public discovery, content, policy, and marketing pages retain the public footer.
* Signed-in workspace roots and their settings/admin branches do not append the public footer.
* A signed-in user visiting a public content route still sees the public footer.
Workspace Empty States:
* Filter-caused empty results explain the active filter and provide a direct clear-filter action.
* Setup-caused empty states pair their explanation with the existing setup or create action.
* Routine zero-item states remain compact and do not grow merely to fill the workspace.
Settings Navigation:
* Settings use concise labels in a desktop rail and scrollable mobile row.
* `/account/settings/profile` is the default; legacy tab inputs only redirect.
* Legacy appearance, language, and preferences values redirect to `/account/settings/preferences`.
* Preferences reuse the pre-paint/localStorage theme mechanism and `/api/account/preferences`; URLs have no locale segments.
* Authorizations remain distinct from linked sign-in accounts and require confirmation before revocation.
* The active section owns its copy, exposes one aria-current page link, and scrolls into view on mobile.
* Danger stays separate from routine settings.
Contribution Heatmap:
* Public profile contribution history keeps a readable minimum cell size and scrolls horizontally when needed.
* Each contribution day exposes its localized date/count to keyboard, touch, and assistive-technology users.
Dashboard Page Identity:
* Every signed-in dashboard branch exposes exactly one localized level-one heading.
* The main content landmark uses the same localized branch label.
* The document title identifies the active branch before the Life@USTC suffix.
* Identity follows the semantic dashboard path after root or query compatibility redirects.
Dashboard Mobile Priority:
* Personal dashboard mobile layouts prioritize the next actionable information before directory-style shortcuts or wide planning grids.
* Calendar uses a readable agenda at mobile widths while retaining semester, month, and week grids on desktop.
* Homework, todo, and exam mobile toolbars keep status filters directly available, move card/list display mode into an accessible overflow menu, and keep primary controls at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels.
Social Sharing Metadata:
* Every rendered page exposes one localized description plus one complete Open Graph and Twitter card set.
* Canonical and Open Graph URLs are absolute, same-origin, and exclude query strings and fragments.
* Course, section, and teacher detail subroutes canonicalize to the entity root and use controlled localized summaries rather than editable user content.
* Social images use the 1200 by 630 Life@USTC PNG card with explicit dimensions, MIME type, and localized alternative text.
Localized Count Copy: * User-visible English count copy distinguishes singular from plural forms; Chinese may share one count template when its grammar does not change.
Navigation Landmarks:
* The desktop application sidebar is a real navigation landmark labelled Primary navigation / 主导航.
* Signed-in mobile layouts expose distinct Mobile primary navigation / 移动主导航 and Secondary navigation / 次级导航 landmarks.
* Each navigation surface exposes at most one aria-current page destination.
* A localized skip link is the first focusable shell control and moves keyboard focus to the main landmark.
* Footer links use one distinct Footer navigation / 页脚导航 landmark so assistive technology can distinguish the regions.
